evidence_quotes.quote: There are very popular so-called "gacha games" from Asia that have similar GLF while having a huge pop culture following (even globally).
games like Genshin Impact or Umamusume Pretty Derby.
Visa and Mastercard have clauses that they can deny service for anything that could be "brand-damaging" (e.g. Mastercard's Rule 5.12.7)
I want DFA to establish that PayPros can't deny to process sales of lawful content.
Visa Japan's CEO has admitted that they're refusing to process payments to legal manga, not because they have to comply with laws, but instead to protect their brand.
This is too much control and should be illegal.
They shouldn't have this much control over the digital market.
They should also not be allowed to refuse global business because of one censorship-prone region.
countries should not be able to push businesses into refusing global business with games, manga or anime (instead of requesting a regional block).
if a piece of media already released in e.g. Germany on one store, then another store shouldn't be able to geo-block it.
This happened to me with games like "Gun Gun Pixies". I can play that game on Nintendo Switch, but not on Steam.
"Blue Archive", it's available on smartphones, but not on Steam.
It's an unfair market to have a PC version be regionally blocked if it's already sold elsewhere.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act has good intentions, but I feel there's a point of weakness. Mainly where it says it will ban games with "gambling-like features" ("GLF"). My idea would be changing it so GLF are treated like real gambling. The reason why is because there are very popular so-called "gacha games" from Asia that have similar GLF while having a huge pop culture following (even globally); games like Genshin Impact or Umamusume Pretty Derby. These gacha games are mainly a financial success in Asia. They could skip the EU and make roughly the same amount of money. If the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative passes, or a clause gets added in here to keep video games in a playable state after the official servers have shut down, then those games that have GLF-based monetization will have their GLF removed after End of Service, and will turn into regular video games without gambling. But if the DFA passes as written, most gacha games will avoid the EU to keep their GLF and because they're not reliant on the EU, thus hindering video game preservation for gacha games after End of Service. If games with GLF were treated like real gambling and if the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative (or something similar) passes: - Western publishers would likely convert their games into something a lot more fair to reach a wider audience, since gambling games would have to be regulated. - Gacha games could stay accessible in the EU, with GLF in them, but they'll have to be regulated like gambling. After End of Service, they'll turn into non-gambling games. Another suggestion, please add a clause to stop payment processors ("PayPros") from denying sales of legal media. Currently, PayPros like Visa/Mastercard are telling partners like PayPal/Stripe to deny sales of video games on Steam and itch.io that include adult-only/18+ content, LGBT or horror content. This is because of Collective Shout from Australia, which has ties to the Australian government. Their co-founder (who's also in the government) founded Womens Forum Australia, which is known for anti-abortion and trans-exclusionary activism. CS has made a public statement where they're proud to have pressured PayPros to go after game stores. In the past, they've also pushed retailers to stop selling games like Grand Theft Auto V and Detroit: Become Human. If this continues, nothing would stop them from telling PayPros to refuse sales of e.g. games with sex appeal, or games with violence in them, or a ban on positive transgender themes. Anything they don't like. This will pressure game creators to self-censor out of fear of losing access to payment services. It's destroying art before it can even exist. Visa Japan's CEO has admitted that they're refusing to process payments to legal manga, not because they have to comply with laws, but instead to protect their brand. This is too much control and should be illegal. Visa and Mastercard have clauses that they can deny service for anything that could be "brand-damaging" (e.g. Mastercard's Rule 5.12.7), without listing everything they reject (with language like "but not limited to"). But even a clear list won't resolve this. I want DFA to establish that PayPros can't deny to process sales of lawful content. They shouldn't have this much control over the digital market. They should also not be allowed to refuse global business because of one censorship-prone region. To future-proof this, countries should not be able to push businesses into refusing global business with games, manga or anime (instead of requesting a regional block). Final suggestion: if a piece of media already released in e.g. Germany on one store, then another store shouldn't be able to geo-block it. This happened to me with games like "Gun Gun Pixies". I can play that game on Nintendo Switch, but not on Steam. "Blue Archive", it's available on smartphones, but not on Steam. It's an unfair market to have a PC version be regionally blocked if it's already sold elsewhere.
